title: Korean Japchae (Glass Noodle Stir-fry)

subs.name: thin rice vermicelli (semiya) soaked in hot water for 5 minutes — gives a similar silky texture and is gluten-free
100 g tofu cubes pan-fried until golden, or torn jackfruit for added bulk
a few drops of roasted peanut oil — different but pleasant nuttiness

steps.text: Soak the glass noodles in a bowl of warm water for 15 minutes until flexible but not fully soft. Drain. Bring a pot of salted water to a boil — you will use it to cook both the noodles and the spinach.

Boil the noodles in the salted water for 2–3 minutes until translucent and just tender. Remove with tongs, rinse immediately under cold water, drain well and toss with 1 tsp gingelly oil and 1 tsp soy sauce to prevent sticking. Use scissors to cut the noodles into 15 cm lengths.
Blanch the spinach in the same boiling water for 30 seconds. Drain, squeeze firmly to remove all water and season with a pinch of salt and a few drops of soy sauce. Set aside.

Cook each vegetable separately in a tsp of oil over high heat to preserve its colour and texture: carrot for 2 minutes with a pinch of salt; mushrooms with 1 clove of minced garlic for 3 minutes until golden; capsicum for 1 minute keeping its crunch. Set each aside separately.

Heat the remaining oil and gingelly oil together in a kadai or wok over high heat. Add the remaining garlic and spring onion whites, stir-fry 30 seconds until fragrant. Add all the cooked vegetables and the noodles to the pan.

Add the remaining soy sauce and the sugar. Toss everything together vigorously for 2 minutes until the noodles are glossy, evenly coated and the sauce has absorbed. Taste for seasoning. Scatter sesame seeds and spring onion greens over the top and serve warm or at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → thin rice vermicelli (semiya) soaked in hot water for 5 minutes — gives a similar silky texture and is gluten-free; mushroom → 100 g tofu cubes pan-fried until golden, or torn jackfruit for added bulk; gingelly-oil → a few drops of roasted peanut oil — different but pleasant nuttiness
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Glass noodles (sweet potato starch noodles) are sold as 'glass noodles' or 'cellophane noodles' in the Chinese or Korean section of Indian metro supermarkets; thin rice semiya (vermicelli) from any Indian provision store makes an equally good substitute. A kadai replaces a wok seamlessly on high flame. Gingelly oil (nallennai in South Indian stores, til ka tel in North India) is the key flavour; mushrooms, carrots and capsicum are available year-round in Indian vegetable markets.
